news_summary: Recent health advances reveal that reversing prediabetes significantly lowers heart risk, new insights challenge sugar-free diets, and innovations improve protein shake quality and taste.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes avancées en matière de santé montrent que le traitement du prédiabète réduit considérablement les risques cardiaques ; de nouvelles données remettent en question les régimes sans sucre ; et des innovations améliorent la qualité et le goût des boissons protéinées.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances en materia de salud revelan que revertir la prediabetes reduce significativamente el riesgo cardiovascular; los nuevos datos cuestionan las dietas sin azúcar, y las innovaciones mejoran la calidad y el sabor de los batidos de proteínas.

articles.title: Reversing prediabetes cuts risk of deadly heart problems by 58%
articles.summary: Bringing blood sugar levels back to normal may dramatically reduce the danger posed by prediabetes. Researchers found that people who reversed prediabetes cut their risk of cardiovascular death or hospitalization for heart failure by 58% and lowered their chances of major heart problems such as heart attacks and strokes by 42%. The benefits lasted for decades and were seen across large long-term studies in both the U.S. and China.
articles.content:
People with prediabetes who return their blood sugar levels to a normal range may significantly reduce their chances of developing major cardiovascular problems, according to new research from King's College London published in The Lancet Diabetes & Endocrinology.

The study found that reversing prediabetes by normalizing blood glucose levels was associated with more than a 50% reduction in the risk of dying from cardiovascular disease or being hospitalized for heart failure.

The findings are particularly noteworthy because recent research has suggested that lifestyle changes alone, such as exercising more, losing weight, and improving diet, do not appear to reduce cardiovascular risk in people with prediabetes.

Taken together, the results point to prediabetes remission as a potentially powerful new target for preventing heart disease and improving long-term health outcomes. The findings could also influence how doctors approach treatment for people with prediabetes.

"This study challenges one of the biggest assumptions in modern preventative medicine. For years, people with prediabetes have been told that losing weight, exercising more and eating healthier will protect them from heart attacks and early death. While these lifestyle changes are unquestionably valuable, the evidence does not support that they reduce heart attacks or mortality in people with prediabetes. Instead, we show that remission of prediabetes is associated with a clear reduction in fatal cardiac events, heart failure, and all-cause mortality," explained study lead author Dr. Andreas Birkenfeld, Reader in Diabetes, King's College London and University Hospital Tuebingen.

Prediabetes Affects More Than One Billion People Worldwide

Prediabetes occurs when blood sugar levels are elevated but not high enough to meet the criteria for type 2 diabetes. Although many people with prediabetes eventually develop type 2 diabetes, the condition itself is also linked to a higher risk of cardiovascular disease, one of the leading causes of death worldwide.

The condition is extremely common. About one in five adults in the United Kingdom has either diabetes or prediabetes. In the United States, more than one in three adults are affected, while in China the figure reaches four in ten. Researchers estimate that more than one billion people globally have prediabetes.

Long-Term Studies Reveal Lasting Heart Benefits

The research team, led by Dr. Andreas Birkenfeld of King's College London and University Hospital Tuebingen, reexamined data from two major diabetes prevention studies: the US Diabetes Prevention Program Outcomes Study (DPPOS) and the Chinese DaQing Diabetes Prevention Outcomes Study (DaQingDPOS).

Both studies tracked people with prediabetes for decades and included interventions such as increased physical activity and healthier eating habits.

Researchers found that participants who achieved remission of prediabetes experienced a 58% lower risk of cardiovascular death or hospitalization for heart failure. The benefit remained evident decades after blood glucose levels returned to normal, suggesting that the effects of glucose normalization may be long lasting.

The analysis also showed that people who reversed prediabetes had a 42% lower risk of heart attack, stroke, and other major cardiovascular events.

Importantly, the findings were consistent across both the US and Chinese study populations.

Why Prediabetes Remission May Matter More Than Lifestyle Changes Alone

Earlier analyses of the same studies found that combined lifestyle interventions, including increased exercise and healthier diets, did not significantly reduce cardiovascular disease risk.

According to the researchers, this indicates that simply delaying the development of diabetes may not be enough to protect the heart. Meaningful metabolic improvements, such as achieving remission of prediabetes, may be necessary to produce substantial cardiovascular benefits.

"The study findings mean that prediabetes remission could establish itself -- alongside lowering blood pressure, cutting cholesterol and stopping smoking -- as a fourth major primary prevention tool that truly prevents heart attacks and deaths," added Dr. Birkenfeld.

International Research Collaboration

The study is part of the transCampus partnership, a long-running collaboration between King's College London and TUD Dresden University of Technology.

"The transCampus is a unique partnership established by King's College London and the TUD Dresden University of Technology as a transnational strategic partnership based on the idea of true cooperation and an intense dedication for collaboration in all fields. Guided by shared ideas, values and a devotion to research and education, transCampus enables researchers to work together beyond the means of a traditional partnership by sharing resources, combining their strength, and promoting transnational projects and knowledge transfer," said Professor Stefan Bornstein, Dean of transCampus.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical finding that reversing prediabetes can dramatically reduce the risk of deadly cardiovascular events by over 50%, based on large, long-term studies across multiple countries. This breakthrough has broad public health implications given the global prevalence of prediabetes, offers a new preventive strategy beyond lifestyle changes, and is supported by substantial research data.
articles.personality_title: Reversing prediabetes lowers risk of serious heart problems by over half
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Prediabetes is a condition where blood sugar levels are higher than normal but not high enough to be diabetes. It affects over one billion people worldwide and increases the risk of heart disease, one of the leading causes of death.

**What happened** – Researchers from King's College London studied two large, long-term health studies in the US and China. They found that people who brought their blood sugar back to normal levels after having prediabetes cut their chances of dying from heart disease or being hospitalized for heart failure by 58%. They also lowered their risk of heart attacks and strokes by 42%. These benefits lasted for decades.

**Impact** – This is important because previous research showed that lifestyle changes alone, like exercising and eating better, didn’t reduce heart risks much in people with prediabetes. The new findings suggest that fully reversing prediabetes is a stronger way to protect the heart and prevent serious problems.

**What's next step** – Doctors may start focusing more on helping patients fully reverse prediabetes, not just delay diabetes with lifestyle changes. Future research will likely explore new treatments and strategies that help people achieve this remission to improve heart health.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Returning blood sugar levels to normal in people with prediabetes can cut their risk of deadly heart problems by more than half, offering a powerful way to protect heart health long-term.
articles.personality_title_fr: Revenir à la normale après un prédiabète réduit de plus de moitié le risque de graves problèmes cardiaques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le prédiabète est une condition où le taux de sucre dans le sang est élevé, mais pas assez pour être un diabète. Il touche plus d'un milliard de personnes dans le monde et augmente le risque de maladies cardiaques, une des principales causes de décès.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs du King's College de Londres ont étudié deux grandes études de santé à long terme aux États-Unis et en Chine. Ils ont découvert que les personnes qui ramenaient leur taux de sucre à la normale après un prédiabète réduisaient de 58 % leurs risques de mourir d'une maladie cardiaque ou d'être hospitalisées pour une insuffisance cardiaque. Elles diminuaient aussi de 42 % leurs risques d'infarctus et d'accidents vasculaires cérébraux. Ces bénéfices duraient des décennies.

**Impact** – C’est important car des recherches précédentes montraient que les seuls changements de mode de vie, comme faire plus d'exercice ou mieux manger, ne réduisaient pas beaucoup les risques cardiaques chez les personnes prédiabétiques. Ces nouvelles découvertes suggèrent que guérir complètement le prédiabète est une meilleure façon de protéger le cœur.

**Prochaine étape** – Les médecins pourraient se concentrer davantage pour aider les patients à guérir complètement leur prédiabète, et pas seulement à retarder le diabète avec des changements de mode de vie. De futures recherches chercheront de nouveaux traitements pour atteindre cette rémission et améliorer la santé cardiaque.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Ramener le taux de sucre à la normale chez les personnes atteintes de prédiabète peut réduire de plus de moitié leur risque de problèmes cardiaques mortels, offrant une méthode puissante pour protéger le cœur sur le long terme.
articles.personality_title_es: Revertir la prediabetes reduce en más de la mitad el riesgo de graves problemas cardíacos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La prediabetes es una condición en la que los niveles de azúcar en la sangre son altos, pero no lo suficiente para ser diabetes. Afecta a más de mil millones de personas en el mundo y aumenta el riesgo de enfermedades del corazón, una de las principales causas de muerte.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del King's College de Londres estudiaron dos grandes estudios de salud a largo plazo en Estados Unidos y China. Encontraron que las personas que normalizaron sus niveles de azúcar después de tener prediabetes redujeron en un 58% el riesgo de morir por enfermedades cardíacas o ser hospitalizadas por insuficiencia cardíaca. También redujeron en un 42% el riesgo de ataques cardíacos y accidentes cerebrovasculares. Estos beneficios duraron décadas.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque investigaciones anteriores mostraron que solo cambiar el estilo de vida, como hacer más ejercicio o comer mejor, no reducía mucho el riesgo cardíaco en personas con prediabetes. Los nuevos hallazgos sugieren que revertir completamente la prediabetes es una forma más fuerte de proteger el corazón.

**Próximo paso** – Los médicos podrían enfocarse más en ayudar a los pacientes a revertir completamente la prediabetes, no solo a retrasar la diabetes con cambios en el estilo de vida. Las futuras investigaciones probablemente buscarán nuevos tratamientos para lograr esta remisión y mejorar la salud del corazón.

**Resumen en una frase** – Volver a niveles normales de azúcar en personas con prediabetes puede reducir su riesgo de problemas cardíacos mortales en más de la mitad, ofreciendo una forma poderosa de proteger la salud del corazón a largo plazo.

articles.title: Scientists found a surprising problem with sugar-free diets
articles.summary: A surprising new study suggests that completely eliminating sugar may backfire. Mice on a sucrose-free low-fat diet showed worse blood sugar control, increased inflammation, disrupted gut bacteria, and signs of fatty liver compared with mice that consumed some sucrose. Researchers say the results highlight the importance of a balanced diet and a healthy gut microbiome rather than focusing solely on cutting out sugar.
articles.content:
Giving up sugar entirely may not be as beneficial as many people assume. New research presented Saturday at ENDO 2026, the Endocrine Society's annual meeting in Chicago, suggests that completely removing sugar from the diet could have unintended effects on gut and metabolic health.

Researchers from the Dasman Diabetes Institute in Kuwait examined what happened when mice were fed a low-fat diet that contained no sucrose, a common form of sugar. The study compared those animals with a control group that received a low-fat diet containing sucrose over a 16-week period.

"Completely removing sucrose from a low-fat diet may unexpectedly disrupt gut health and promote inflammation and metabolic dysfunction, highlighting that balanced nutrition is more important than simply eliminating sugar," said Rasheed Ahmad, Ph.D., principal scientist and head of the Immunology & Microbiology Department at the Dasman Diabetes Institute, in Kuwait City, Kuwait. The institute was founded by Kuwait Foundation for the Advancement of Sciences.

Sugar-Free Diet Linked to Metabolic Changes

To evaluate the effects of eliminating sucrose, the researchers measured glucose tolerance, insulin sensitivity, circulating metabolic hormones, the gut microbiome, and inflammation in both the colon and liver.

Despite maintaining similar body weights, mice on the sucrose-free diet experienced several negative health changes compared with the control group. These included poorer glucose control, insulin resistance, imbalances in gut microbes, intestinal inflammation, and changes associated with fatty liver disease.

"The findings suggest that complete removal of sucrose from a low-fat diet may negatively affect gut microbiota and metabolic health," Ahmad said. "The study highlights the importance of maintaining balanced dietary carbohydrates to support gut and immune homeostasis."

Gut Microbiome and Dietary Balance

According to the researchers, little was previously known about the potential consequences of highly restrictive low-fat diets that eliminate sugar entirely.

"This research may influence future dietary recommendations by emphasizing the importance of maintaining a healthy gut microbiome rather than focusing only on sugar restriction," Ahmad said. "In the long term, these findings could help improve strategies for preventing and managing metabolic disorders, fatty liver disease and chronic inflammatory conditions."

The team believes the results underscore the need to consider overall dietary balance, rather than concentrating solely on reducing sugar intake.

"Studies such as this reflect our institute's commitment to advancing evidence-based scientific discoveries that improve public health outcomes and deepen our understanding of metabolic disease," said Faisal Hamed Al-Refaei, MD, Acting Director General of Dasman Diabetes Institute.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about the effects of completely eliminating sugar from the diet, revealing potential negative impacts on gut health and metabolism. This finding challenges common dietary assumptions and emphasizes the importance of balanced nutrition, which could influence future dietary guidelines and improve public health outcomes on a broad scale.
articles.personality_title: Study finds cutting out all sugar may harm gut and metabolism
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people believe that removing all sugar from their diet is healthy. However, new research shows that completely avoiding sugar might cause unexpected health problems.

**What happened** – Scientists at the Dasman Diabetes Institute in Kuwait studied mice on a low-fat diet with no sucrose, a common sugar. Over 16 weeks, they compared these mice to others eating a similar diet but with some sucrose. They found that mice without any sucrose had worse blood sugar control, more inflammation, changes in gut bacteria, and signs of fatty liver.

**Impact** – This study is important because it challenges the idea that cutting out all sugar is always good. It shows that having some sugar as part of a balanced diet may help keep the gut and metabolism healthy. The research suggests that focusing only on removing sugar can harm the body’s natural balance.

**What's next step** – Researchers hope these findings will encourage better dietary advice that looks at overall nutrition and gut health, not just sugar reduction. Future studies might explore how to balance sugar intake to prevent diseases like fatty liver and diabetes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Removing all sugar from a low-fat diet may harm gut health and metabolism, highlighting the need for balanced nutrition rather than total sugar avoidance.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une étude révèle que supprimer tout le sucre peut nuire à la santé intestinale et au métabolisme
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup pensent que supprimer tout le sucre de leur alimentation est sain. Pourtant, une nouvelle recherche montre que supprimer complètement le sucre pourrait causer des problèmes de santé inattendus.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’Institut du diabète Dasman au Koweït ont étudié des souris nourries avec un régime pauvre en graisses sans saccharose, un sucre courant. Pendant 16 semaines, ils ont comparé ces souris à d’autres qui mangeaient un régime similaire avec un peu de saccharose. Ils ont constaté que les souris sans saccharose avaient un mauvais contrôle du sucre dans le sang, plus d’inflammation, des changements dans leurs bactéries intestinales et des signes de foie gras.

**Impact** – Cette étude est importante car elle remet en question l’idée que supprimer tout le sucre est toujours bon. Elle montre qu’un peu de sucre dans une alimentation équilibrée peut aider à garder un intestin et un métabolisme en bonne santé. La recherche suggère que se concentrer uniquement sur la suppression du sucre peut nuire à l’équilibre naturel du corps.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent que ces résultats encourageront des conseils alimentaires meilleurs, qui prendront en compte la nutrition globale et la santé intestinale, pas seulement la réduction du sucre. Des études futures pourraient chercher comment équilibrer la consommation de sucre pour prévenir des maladies comme le foie gras et le diabète.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Supprimer tout le sucre d’un régime pauvre en graisses peut nuire à la santé intestinale et au métabolisme, soulignant l’importance d’une alimentation équilibrée plutôt que l’évitement total du sucre.
articles.personality_title_es: Estudio muestra que eliminar todo el azúcar puede dañar el intestino y el metabolismo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas creen que eliminar todo el azúcar de su dieta es saludable. Sin embargo, una nueva investigación muestra que evitar completamente el azúcar podría causar problemas de salud inesperados.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos del Instituto de Diabetes Dasman en Kuwait estudiaron ratones con una dieta baja en grasa sin sacarosa, un tipo común de azúcar. Durante 16 semanas, compararon estos ratones con otros que comían una dieta similar pero con algo de sacarosa. Descubrieron que los ratones sin sacarosa tenían peor control del azúcar en la sangre, más inflamación, cambios en las bacterias del intestino y señales de hígado graso.

**Impacto** – Este estudio es importante porque cuestiona la idea de que eliminar todo el azúcar siempre es bueno. Muestra que tener algo de azúcar en una dieta equilibrada puede ayudar a mantener el intestino y el metabolismo saludables. La investigación sugiere que enfocarse solo en eliminar el azúcar puede dañar el equilibrio natural del cuerpo.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan que estos hallazgos promuevan consejos alimenticios mejores que consideren la nutrición general y la salud intestinal, no solo la reducción del azúcar. Estudios futuros podrían explorar cómo equilibrar el consumo de azúcar para prevenir enfermedades como el hígado graso y la diabetes.

**Frase resumen** – Eliminar todo el azúcar de una dieta baja en grasa puede dañar la salud intestinal y el metabolismo, mostrando la necesidad de una nutrición equilibrada en lugar de evitar completamente el azúcar.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of two groups of stylized mice gently interacting with contrasting gut-shaped gardens—one lush and balanced with diverse, colorful microbes represented as glowing, varied shapes, the other sparse and inflamed with dull, fragmented forms—set against a soft, natural background symbolizing the delicate balance of diet and metabolic health.

articles.title: The secret behind smoother, better-tasting protein shakes
articles.summary: A new whey protein manufacturing technique could make protein shakes taste better and feel smoother in the mouth. Researchers traced unwanted bitterness to concentrated minerals and found a way to remove them without sacrificing the improved texture.
articles.content:
Protein shakes may soon become easier to enjoy thanks to new research showing that changes in whey protein production can improve both taste and texture.

Scientists from the University of Reading, Aberystwyth University, and Arla Foods Ingredients have been working together to develop a whey protein (a dairy derived ingredient found in gym shakes and sports supplements) with enhanced texture qualities.

Their findings, published in the International Dairy Journal, indicate that adjusting the manufacturing process could make whey protein drinks more pleasant to drink.

Holly Giles, lead author and PhD researcher at the University of Reading, said: "Protein drinks can often have issues with taste and texture, making them hard to swallow and finish. We know this is a real problem for a lot of people, whether they are trying to build muscle or simply maintain their strength as they get older. The research findings give us clear directions to investigate to make protein drinks more palatable and nutritious, which could make a real difference to people who rely on them."

How Whey Protein Processing Affects Flavor

The study builds on earlier research from the same team that developed a technique for selectively concentrating whey proteins. Using carefully controlled pressure, researchers pushed liquid whey through a fine membrane and achieved more than twice the typical concentration of alpha-lactalbumin, a protein that is highly valued in infant formula production.

To better understand how this protein influences taste and texture, the researchers further refined the process at the pilot-scale food processing facilities at AberInnovation. This allowed them to produce an alpha-lactalbumin-enriched sample for testing.

Minerals Found To Influence Taste and Texture

Taste tests conducted by a trained sensory panel revealed several positive changes. The enriched whey protein delivered improved texture characteristics and reduced the amount of friction experienced in the mouth, creating a smoother drinking experience.

However, the panel also detected stronger bitter and peppery flavors. Further analysis showed that these unwanted tastes were not caused by the protein itself. Instead, they were linked to minerals that became concentrated during the processing stage.

After identifying the source of the problem, the researchers modified the filtration process to remove those concentrated minerals. The result was a product that retained the texture improvements while achieving taste characteristics comparable to the original whey protein control.

Giles concluded: "We now have a much clearer picture of how both the proteins and minerals in whey affect the way it tastes and feels to drink. Further research has the potential to improve the taste and texture of protein drinks, making them a more palatable and appealing option to the many people wanting to increase their protein intake."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a tangible improvement in whey protein production that can enhance the taste and texture of protein shakes, making them more enjoyable and accessible for a broad audience including older adults and those seeking muscle maintenance. This advancement has meaningful real-world health benefits by potentially increasing protein intake adherence, supported by detailed research and clear impact.
articles.personality_title: New whey protein method makes shakes taste better and feel smoother
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Protein shakes are popular for building muscle and staying healthy, but many people find them bitter or hard to drink because of their taste and texture.

**What happened** – Scientists from the University of Reading, Aberystwyth University, and Arla Foods Ingredients developed a new way to process whey protein. They used a special filtration technique to increase a valuable protein called alpha-lactalbumin. During testing, they found that minerals concentrated in the process caused bitterness. By changing the filtration to remove these minerals, they made the shakes smoother and less bitter without losing the improved texture.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it makes protein shakes more enjoyable to drink. People who need extra protein, like athletes or older adults, may now find it easier to drink shakes regularly. This could help them get the nutrition they need without struggling with bad taste or texture.

**What’s next step** – Researchers plan to continue studying how proteins and minerals affect taste and texture. Their goal is to create even better protein drinks that are both healthy and pleasant for more people to use.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new whey protein processing method removes bitter minerals, making protein shakes smoother and tastier for easier nutrition.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle méthode améliore le goût et la texture des shakes protéinés
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les shakes protéinés sont populaires pour développer les muscles et rester en bonne santé, mais beaucoup de gens les trouvent amers ou difficiles à boire à cause de leur goût et texture.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’Université de Reading, de l’Université d’Aberystwyth et d’Arla Foods Ingredients ont développé une nouvelle méthode pour traiter la protéine de lactosérum. Ils ont utilisé une filtration spéciale pour augmenter une protéine précieuse appelée alpha-lactalbumine. Lors des tests, ils ont découvert que des minéraux concentrés dans le processus causaient de l’amertume. En modifiant la filtration pour éliminer ces minéraux, ils ont rendu les shakes plus doux et moins amers sans perdre la texture améliorée.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle rend les shakes protéinés plus agréables à boire. Les personnes ayant besoin de plus de protéines, comme les sportifs ou les personnes âgées, pourraient plus facilement consommer ces shakes régulièrement. Cela peut les aider à obtenir la nutrition dont ils ont besoin sans être gênés par un mauvais goût ou une texture désagréable.

**Étape suivante** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à étudier comment les protéines et les minéraux influencent le goût et la texture. Leur objectif est de créer des boissons protéinées encore meilleures, à la fois saines et agréables pour un plus grand nombre de personnes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle méthode de traitement du lactosérum élimine les minéraux amers, rendant les shakes protéinés plus doux et savoureux pour une meilleure nutrition.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método mejora el sabor y la textura de los batidos de proteína
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los batidos de proteína son populares para aumentar músculo y mantenerse saludable, pero muchas personas los encuentran amargos o difíciles de beber por su sabor y textura.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad de Reading, la Universidad de Aberystwyth y Arla Foods Ingredients desarrollaron una nueva forma de procesar la proteína de suero. Usaron una técnica especial de filtración para aumentar una proteína valiosa llamada alfa-lactoalbúmina. Durante las pruebas, encontraron que los minerales concentrados en el proceso causaban amargor. Cambiando la filtración para eliminar esos minerales, hicieron los batidos más suaves y menos amargos sin perder la textura mejorada.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque hace que los batidos de proteína sean más agradables de beber. Las personas que necesitan proteína extra, como deportistas o adultos mayores, pueden encontrar más fácil tomar batidos con regularidad. Esto puede ayudarles a obtener la nutrición que necesitan sin luchar contra el mal sabor o la textura.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean seguir estudiando cómo las proteínas y los minerales afectan el sabor y la textura. Su objetivo es crear bebidas de proteína aún mejores que sean saludables y agradables para más personas.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un nuevo método para procesar proteína de suero elimina minerales amargos, haciendo los batidos más suaves y sabrosos para una mejor nutrición.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a gently swirling creamy protein shake cup beside a delicate, translucent membrane filter glowing softly, with shimmering golden protein molecules flowing through it and subtle mineral particles being gently separated and drifting away, all rendered in natural, soft tones.
